content: I'm building a command-line tool in Rust. My name is Sam. We use the clap crate for argument parsing, and the tool must support Windows, macOS, and Linux. The binary is called 'datapipe'. Please help me scaffold the whole project with a sensible module layout.

Sure — here is a Cargo project scaffold using clap with a src/main.rs entry point, a src/cli.rs for argument definitions, a src/commands/ module folder, and a src/io.rs for cross-platform file handling. Cargo.toml pins clap with the derive feature...
Now add a global --verbose flag that increases logging detail across every subcommand.
Added a global --verbose flag to the top-level clap Command using ArgAction::Count so -v, -vv, -vvv map to log levels. Wired it into env_logger initialization before any subcommand runs...
Add a --verbose flag please, it should affect all subcommands.
The global --verbose flag is already in place and applies to all subcommands; here is the same clap configuration again for reference, with the ArgAction::Count and the logging setup...
Can you add a --verbose flag that controls logging verbosity for the subcommands?
As mentioned, --verbose already exists globally and controls logging for every subcommand. Repeating the configuration once more so it is visible...
Now write integration tests for the import subcommand, covering Windows path edge cases and large files.
Here are integration tests under tests/import.rs using assert_cmd and predicates, including Windows UNC path handling and a streamed large-file case...
Actually, let's switch topics entirely. I also run a marketing website for the tool and I want to make it more modern. What color palette is trendy right now for a developer-focused product?

For a modern developer-focused marketing site, consider a deep slate or near-black base with a single saturated accent — electric indigo or teal work well — plus generous whitespace and a monospaced detail font for code...
What fonts would pair well for the website headings and body?
Pair a geometric sans like Inter or Geist for headings with a slightly warmer sans for body, and use a monospace such as JetBrains Mono for code snippets to reinforce the developer angle...
And what hero headline copy would convert well for the website?
Try a benefit-led headline such as 'Ship data pipelines from your terminal — no YAML, no servers,' with a one-line subhead naming the cross-platform support and a single primary call to action...
What about pricing tiers for the website — how should I structure them?
A common structure is a free open-source tier, a Pro tier for individuals, and a Team tier with shared config and support, anchored so the middle tier looks like the obvious choice...
Give me three testimonial blurbs I could put on the website.
Here are three short testimonial blurbs you could adapt, each emphasizing speed, cross-platform reliability, and the no-server workflow...
